Z	e	
z~Abstract Base Classes (ABCs) for numbers, according to PEP 3141.
TODO: Fill out more detailed documentation on the operators.
ABCMeta
abstractmethod)
Number
Complex
Real
Rational
Integralc
All numbers inherit from this class.
    If you just want to check if an argument x is a number, without
    caring what kind, use isinstance(x, Number).
    
__name__
__module__
__qualname__
__doc__
	__slots__
__hash__r	
/usr/lib64/python3.9/numbers.pyr
	metaclassc
d!d"
d#d$
d%d&
d'd(
Complex defines the operations that work on the builtin complex type.
    In short, those are: a conversion to complex, .real, .imag, +, -,
    *, /, **, abs(), .conjugate, ==, and !=.
    If it is given heterogeneous arguments, and doesn't have special
    knowledge about them, it should fall back to the builtin complex
    type as described below.
    r	
z<Return a builtin complex instance. Called for complex(self).Nr	
selfr	
__complex__-
Complex.__complex__c
z)True if self != 0. Called for bool(self).r
__bool__1
Complex.__bool__c
zXRetrieve the real component of this number.
        This should subclass Real.
        N
NotImplementedErrorr
real5
Complex.realc
z]Retrieve the imaginary component of this number.
        This should subclass Real.
        Nr
imag>
Complex.imagc
self + otherNr
otherr

other + selfNr

z5self / other: Should promote to float when necessary.Nr

z7Returns the Real distance from 0. Called for abs(self).Nr
__abs__
Complex.__abs__c
z$(x+y*i).conjugate() returns (x-y*i).Nr
	conjugate
Complex.conjugatec
self == otherNr
__eq__
Complex.__eq__N)
propertyr
d d!
d"d#
d$d%
To Complex, Real adds the operations that work on real numbers.
    In short, those are: a conversion to float, trunc(), divmod,
    %, <, <=, >, and >=.
    Real also provides defaults for the derived operations.
    r	
zTAny Real can be converted to a native float object.
        Called for float(self).Nr
	__float__
Real.__float__c
trunc(self): Truncates self to an Integral.
        Returns an Integral i such that:
          * i>0 iff self>0;
          * abs(i) <= abs(self);
          * for any Integral j satisfying the first two conditions,
            abs(i) >= abs(j) [i.e. i has "maximal" abs among those].
        i.e. "truncate towards 0".
        Nr
	__trunc__
Real.__trunc__c
z$Finds the greatest Integral <= self.Nr
	__floor__
Real.__floor__c
z!Finds the least Integral >= self.Nr
__ceil__
Real.__ceil__Nc
Rounds self to ndigits decimal places, defaulting to 0.
        If ndigits is omitted or None, returns an Integral, otherwise
        returns a Real. Rounds half toward even.
        Nr
ndigitsr	
	__round__
Real.__round__c
divmod(self, other): The pair (self // other, self % other).
        Sometimes this can be computed faster than the pair of
        operations.
        r	
__divmod__
Real.__divmod__c
divmod(other, self): The pair (self // other, self % other).
        Sometimes this can be computed faster than the pair of
        operations.
        r	
__rdivmod__
Real.__rdivmod__c
z)self // other: The floor() of self/other.Nr
__floordiv__
Real.__floordiv__c
z)other // self: The floor() of other/self.Nr
__rfloordiv__
Real.__rfloordiv__c
self % otherNr
__mod__
Real.__mod__c
other % selfNr
__rmod__
Real.__rmod__c
zRself < other
        < on Reals defines a total ordering, except perhaps for NaN.Nr
__lt__
Real.__lt__c
self <= otherNr
__le__
Real.__le__c
z(complex(self) == complex(float(self), 0))
complex
floatr
Real.__complex__c
z&Real numbers are their real component.r	
z	Real.realc
z)Real numbers have no imaginary component.r
z	Real.imagc
Conjugate is a no-op for Reals.r	
Real.conjugate)
Z	d	S
z6.numerator and .denominator should be in lowest terms.r	
	numerator
Rational.numeratorc
denominator
Rational.denominatorc
float(self) = self.numerator / self.denominator
        It's important that this conversion use the integer's "true"
        division rather than casting one side to float before dividing
        so that ratios of huge integers convert without overflowing.
        )
Rational.__float__N)
d d!
d"d#
d$d%
Integral adds methods that work on integral numbers.
    In short, these are conversion to int, pow with modulus, and the
    bit-string operations.
    r	
z	int(self)Nr
__int__/
Integral.__int__c
z6Called whenever an index is needed, such as in slicing)
intr
	__index__4
Integral.__index__Nc
self ** exponent % modulus, but maybe faster.
        Accept the modulus argument if you want to support the
        3-argument version of pow(). Raise a TypeError if exponent < 0
        or any argument isn't Integral. Otherwise, just implement the
        2-argument version described in Complex.
        Nr
modulusr	
Integral.__pow__c
self << otherNr
__lshift__C
Integral.__lshift__c
other << selfNr
__rlshift__H
Integral.__rlshift__c
self >> otherNr
__rshift__M
Integral.__rshift__c
other >> selfNr
__rrshift__R
Integral.__rrshift__c
self & otherNr
__and__W
Integral.__and__c
other & selfNr
__rand__\
Integral.__rand__c
self ^ otherNr
__xor__a
Integral.__xor__c
other ^ selfNr
__rxor__f
Integral.__rxor__c
self | otherNr
__or__k
Integral.__or__c
other | selfNr
__ror__p
Integral.__ror__c
~selfNr
__invert__u
Integral.__invert__c
float(self) == float(int(self)))
Integral.__float__c
z"Integers are their own numerators.r	
Integral.numeratorc
z!Integers have a denominator of 1.
Integral.denominator)
